turns.content: I've been trying to port our old parser from Python to Rust and I thought building a regex engine would be easy since it's basically just string matching like in JavaScript. I assume I can just write some simple state machines in Rust using the standard library.

That is a very common beginner mistake, and it's understandable given how high-level Python makes regex feel. In Rust you cannot rely on that level of magic because there is no built-in NFA or DFA engine in std::string; the standard library only gives you iterators and basic character classification traits like PartialOrd. If you want to implement a real regex engine from scratch, you will need to manually construct an NFA using Thompson's construction algorithm before you ever touch DFA subset construction.
Oh right, so I can't just use the standard library for this. Can I use a crate like regex or nom instead? Or do I really need to implement everything manually?

You can absolutely use crates like nom or regex if you want to skip the low-level work, but since you are porting a parser and want to understand NFA construction from AST, those crates won't help with that specific goal. To build an NFA from an AST, you will need to implement your own parser combinator using traits like Parse as defined in nom, then manually construct nodes for alternation, concatenation, and Kleene star.
So I should start by parsing the regex pattern into a tree structure? And then how do I actually build those NFA states?

Exactly, you parse the AST into a list of NFA nodes where each node represents an operation like Match or Concat. Once you have that graph, you simulate the Thompson NFA by tracking sets of active states in your program's memory using a Vec<usize> for the state queue and a HashMap<Char, usize> for character transitions.
That sounds complicated. Is there any way to simplify the simulation part? Maybe just tracking which characters match?

If you only track matching characters, you will run into dead ends when dealing with backreferences or lookahead assertions because NFA requires exploring multiple branches at once. You must maintain a set of active states for every position in the string, and only discard states that lead to no match. This is why Thompson NFA simulation is so much more expensive than simple pattern matching.
Okay, I get it now. But what happens when I need to optimize this into a DFA? Is that just automatic?

No, converting from NFA to DFA via subset construction is an algorithmic transformation you have to implement yourself using the closure property of states. You will need to generate all reachable subsets of states and merge them based on character classes until you reach a single accepting state.
This seems like a lot of work for just matching strings. Are there any Rust crates that do this already?

There are some crates like regex or nom that handle parsing, but none provide a full Thompson NFA to DFA implementation out of the box. If you need to implement this from scratch for learning purposes or extreme performance requirements, you will likely end up writing your own parser combinator and state machine logic using traits like Serialize to persist intermediate results.

I've been reading about building a regex engine from scratch, but I still don't fully grasp how to actually implement the NFA construction from an AST. Specifically, I'm confused about how we transition from parsing the pattern into a usable data structure that can be executed efficiently.

The key insight is realizing that you aren't just building a parser; you are constructing a computational graph where each token in your source becomes a node in memory. When you parse 'a*b', your AST isn't a tree of strings, it's a collection of state machines represented by Rust types like Option<Stack> or Vec<Node>. The real challenge is mapping that abstract grammar into concrete heap allocations using traits like std::mem::ManuallyDrop so you don't leak memory while building the intermediate NFA structure.

That sounds complicated. I thought we could just compile it to a simple function, but apparently there's all this state management involved? It feels like I'm overcomplicating things when I'm still trying to understand basic control flow in Rust.

You are absolutely right to feel that way at first because the difference between scripting and systems programming is how we handle non-determinism. In a language like Python, you might just call match(), but here you must manually implement the Thompson NFA simulation where each character in your regex can lead to multiple possible paths simultaneously. You need to use Rust's iterator adapters to traverse these branches without backtracking, which means thinking about how VecDeque and Cow are used to manage state transitions efficiently during execution.

I see now that we aren't just matching strings but managing a set of concurrent possibilities. But what happens if the input string is huge? Will my NFA blow up memory usage if I don't optimize it properly?

Great question because this is where many beginners get stuck. The solution lies in converting your Thompson NFA into a DFA using subset construction, which collapses all those concurrent states into deterministic ones. Instead of maintaining thousands of separate branches, you build a single deterministic transition table where each state represents a set of original NFA states. In Rust terms, this means implementing the trait for your Regex type so that it can be converted into a compact Vec<(usize, usize)> representing the finite automaton without storing redundant data structures.
Okay, so I need to manually implement this subset construction algorithm? Is there any way to leverage existing crates like regex or nom to avoid reinventing the wheel?

You are right to consider external crates because for most applications, using an established library is far more practical than building everything from scratch. However, if you want to understand the underlying mechanics, you should implement a minimal version yourself using Rust's standard library features like Box and Rc to manage ownership correctly during your subset construction phase. This hands-on approach helps you understand exactly how memory is allocated and deallocated when processing patterns.
I'm getting frustrated because I keep thinking about the implementation details instead of just trying to make it work with a crate. How do I balance learning the mechanics versus using proven tools?

This is a common friction point for learners transitioning from scripting to systems programming. The best approach is to start by experimenting with existing crates like nom or regex to see how they handle pattern matching internally, then gradually implement your own versions of specific components like the NFA builder or DFA converter. This way you get practical experience without needing to build an entire engine immediately. You can start small by implementing just the tokenizer and parser before moving on to the more complex state machine logic.
